# Transcrição: 17-Penny Drive

- **Fonte:** 17-Penny Drive
- **Duração:** 06:24
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

## [00:00 - 03:02]

Yeah, keep it going for Matthew! Hi! Hi! Hi! I do know how to work at. Thanks. I'm in my sixth year of teaching. I'm sitting in an assembly when I hear the principal announce that we're going to be doing a penny drive for autism awareness. This is a fabulous organization and we have special needs kids in our class. I have two autistic kids in my class, but it's like a penny drive. Like it's just not very inspired. The autistic kid next to me isn't even inspired by this autism speaks penny drive. But then the principal says the class that collects the most pennies will win a pizza party. And like every kid shoots up because I've been teaching 18 years and I know that you can get kids to do anything for food. They're like one step from trained seals. They will do anything for any caloric intake whatsoever. And so they want to win and I want to win because I like to win. And I'm not afraid to take credit for my students' achievements. Now I know that we're going to need a plan because I'm teaching third grade and there are kindergartners over there who are enthusiastic and excited and they've got helicopter parents and they're unstoppable. And I am teaching third grade and by third grade kids are apathetic and they are lost. They are like shambling zombies just walking to their death. The life is over for them and as much as they want the pizza I know they can't collect as many pennies as those little sons of bitches over there. But I have a plan. I know a way to do this. So the penny drive takes off and I wait three days. And on the third day before the kids go to recess I gather them and I say I have a plan. This is how we're going to win. I say listen when I was a kid I fought a lot. Like real fights like you punch people in the face and some kid raises his hand like weren't you labeled a bully. I'm like there were no bullies back then. There was blood and there was teeth and there was fists. And I won all of those fights and they look at me and they're like there's no way in hell you want to fight buddy. And I say no I did. And it's true. What I used to do is I would always let the guy punch me first. And no matter how much it hurt no matter how days I was no matter how much blood was in my mouth from like when your lips connect to your teeth I would never show pain. I would just smile at the guy. I learned that when you take away someone's confidence like there are hopes and dreams you can beat anybody. And so I tell the kids this is what we're going to do. I say you're going to go out at recess and you're going to tell every kid that we already have three jars of pennies. And some little girl's like but we don't I know like we know we don't. You're going to lie you're going to go out there and you're going to destroy the hopes and dreams of kids. And they do. They go out and they tell everyone. And this river of pennies that has been flowing into the school becomes this trickle. And like we are going to win. And then this unexpected thing happens. The kids in the other classes their teachers say why aren't

## [03:02 - 06:03]

you bringing in pennies. And they say well Mr. Dix's class already has three jars full. And teachers do what kids do best. They tattle on me. The teachers go to the principal and they say Matt claims to have three jars of pennies are kids have quit. And so the principal shows up at my door one day. And he says something that like it strikes a dagger in my heart. He says show me those three jars of pennies. And I say you see I don't have three jars of pennies. But what I had was the teachable moment. And I tell him what I did. I say listen I wanted the kids to win. And I taught them how to strip the hopes and dreams from little kids and the punches and the blood and the teeth. And I sent them out. And he listens and I'm waiting for like understanding to like wash over his face. But nothing like that happens. Instead he says to me let's put aside the fact that you taught kids a lesson that is highly questionable about. But do you think it was really like the moment like you chose the autism speaks charity event to teach the kids this lesson. And I say like I think it was the wrong time to teach these kids this lesson. But sometimes the wrong time is the best time. And sometimes it's the only time. And you'll be surprised to discover the principal does not buy this like this is not a reasonable argument for this man. And so he tells me I need to go and tell all the teachers what I've done and the kids have to go. And we do. We call it all off. But we still win. Because it turns out that when you strip the hopes and dreams from people they don't come back. Like you can bury kindergartners in pain and suffering and they will never rise up again. And my kids get the pizza party and we win the penny drive. But what I don't tell the principal is as much as I wanted to win and I truly did. I didn't teach my kids that lesson because pizza. There's this amazing thing and it's true in teaching and like you don't believe it unless you're a teacher. But when you get your list of kids in August and it's your name on the top and there's 20 names below it even though you have never set eyes on those 20 kids you instantly love those kids more than anyone else in the school. You love them a tick away from your own kids. And you want those kids to be the best they can be. And if you love someone you know that sometimes the lessons you teach people they're not the most noble lessons. They're not. And they're not the the goodest lessons in the world. They're not even sometimes the right lessons. But I know that these kids these 20 kids who I love just by looking at their names that they're going to go into really a cruel and difficult and sometimes terrible world and as awful a lesson as I taught those kids I know that sometimes you need that lesson that you need terrible lessons

## [06:04 - 06:24]

to get through this world. I did it because I love them and I know that sounds like a ridiculous thing but it is a true thing. It's the same lesson I'm going to teach my own kids someday that sometimes the way you win is you knock other people down and you climb right over them. Thank you.
